text: The surgeon places a 10mm camera port near the umbilicus and additional 10mm and 5mm ports on the right and left sides for dissection and suturing. A 5mm port from the left subcostal region is used to retract the liver upwards. During diagnostic laparoscopy, the collection of bile in the right subhepatic space is aspirated, and fibrinous flakes are separated using a suction cannula to identify the perforation site. A U-stitch is applied in the falciform area to retract the liver and expose the perforation site.

The surgeon gently removes slough around a small perforation to accurately assess its size. An inferior stitch is placed approximately one centimeter away from the perforation using 2-0 bursal sutures, followed by a middle stitch. The sutures are aligned along the long axis of the perforation, preparing for the application of a Gram's patch using live omentum.

The surgeon places the superior-most stitch using the Zaboos technique, ensuring the suture is snugly fitted to avoid ischemia of the omentum while preventing bile leakage. The live omental patch is positioned over the ulcer perforation, which measures approximately eight millimeters, with sutures oriented along the long axis of the duodenum.

The surgeon applies pressure to secure the knot on the inferior suture of the omental patch, ensuring it is tension-free. The head of the patient is positioned lower to facilitate proper suturing without tension on the omentum. The knot is held tightly for a few seconds to ensure it remains secure and does not slip.

The surgeon snugly fits the middle suture, completing the three sutures for the ulcer repair. A cavity wash is performed in Morrison's pouch in the right subhepatic space, with the patient in a head-low position to prevent fluid from spreading throughout the abdomen. Fibrinous flakes are gently peeled off to avoid postoperative adhesions. The camera is repositioned to access the pelvis, where bile leakage is observed, and an extreme head-low position is used to facilitate washing the pelvic area.

The surgeon gently lifts the sigmoid colon to access the rectovesical pouch, performing irrigation with normal saline until clear fluid is observed. A drain is placed through one of the ports into the pelvis. The surgeon then returns to the upper abdomen, checking for bile leakage and ensuring biliostasis. The omentum appears healthy, well-perfused, and free of ischemia or bile stains.

The surgeon applies abyssal glue to the ulcer site, followed by placing a second layer of omentum over the glued area for additional security. The procedure is performed through a five-millimeter port, which is enlarged to accommodate the placement of a drain.
